Name: adaptive_kernel_tuning
Title: Adaptive Kernel Tuning for Efficient Inference on Commodity GPUs
Short Hypothesis: Can dynamic profiling of kernel execution characteristics during inference lead to significant optimizations in real-time by adaptively tuning kernel parameters on-the-fly? This approach is necessary as static tuning often fails to capture the variability in workload characteristics and system states, which are crucial for maximizing throughput and minimizing latency on commodity hardware.
Related Work: Existing work has focused on static optimization strategies or hardware-agnostic frameworks for deep learning inference (e.g., Unified Operator Fusion and CAGR). However, these approaches often overlook the dynamism inherent in kernel execution, particularly in real-world scenarios where workload characteristics change rapidly due to variations in input data and hardware states. This proposal distinguishes itself by emphasizing real-time, adaptive tuning based on live profiling data, which has not been sufficiently explored in the context of kernel optimization.
Abstract: This proposal aims to explore a novel approach to kernel optimization for deep learning inference on commodity GPUs through real-time adaptive tuning. By leveraging dynamic profiling techniques to monitor kernel execution characteristics—such as occupancy, memory bandwidth, and stall reasons—we will develop a framework that adjusts kernel parameters on-the-fly to optimize performance based on the current workload. This adaptive tuning mechanism will not only enhance throughput and reduce latency but also account for the variability in computational requirements inherent in diverse neural network architectures and input data distributions. We plan to implement this approach within an existing deep learning inference engine, utilizing CUDA and Triton to showcase its effectiveness. Our evaluation metrics will include p50/p99 latency, throughput under batching, and energy per token, with results benchmarked against static tuning methods. This research will contribute to the development of more efficient deep learning systems, paving the way for practical deployment in resource-constrained environments.
Experiments: 1. Implement a baseline static kernel tuning strategy for a set of common deep learning models (e.g., ResNet, BERT) on a commodity GPU. 2. Develop the adaptive kernel tuning framework that utilizes profiling data to adjust parameters during inference. 3. Conduct A/B testing between the static and adaptive systems, measuring p50/p99 latency and throughput under batching. 4. Evaluate energy consumption per inference and analyze the impact of adaptive tuning on different network architectures. 5. Perform ablation studies to isolate the effects of specific tuning parameters and profiling features on overall performance.
Risk Factors and Limitations: 1. Complexity in implementation may lead to inconsistencies in tuning decisions if not managed properly. 2. Dependence on accurate profiling data may introduce overhead, potentially negating performance benefits in low-latency scenarios. 3. The performance gains may not generalize across all types of models or input distributions, necessitating additional tuning for specific applications.
